title: Preisach Attention: A Hysteretic Model of Sequential Memory
abstract: We introduce the Preisach Attention Layer (PAL), a novel sequence modelling architecture grounded in the classical Preisach hysteresis operator from mathematical physics. PAL replaces the softmax attention mechanism with a binary relay operator parameterised by learned activation and deactivation thresholds, maintaining a stack of local extrema as its internal state. A single-layer PAL-Transformer with O(1) depth is Turing-complete under arbitrary precision arithmetic, achievable through simulation of a two-stack pushdown automaton -- in contrast to the O(log n) depth required by standard hard-attention transformers. Second, we prove that the function classes computable by PAL and by the transformer are incomparable: PAL computes historical range statistics in O(1) layers that require O(log n) layers for transformers, while transformers support random-access retrieval that PAL cannot perform without auxiliary state. The separating property is rate-independence -- PAL responds only to the sequence of local extrema, not to absolute token positions or temporal spacing. Third, we show that the extremum stack constitutes a minimal sufficient statistic of the input history for all rate-independent functionals, providing a formal analogue of the wiping property in classical hysteresis theory. PAL is thus an efficient architecture for tasks with long episodic memory and weak positional dependence, with O(n log n) total inference cost versus O(n^2) for standard attention.
summary: This paper introduces a new neural network attention mechanism based on the physics of hysteresis (the Preisach model). It demonstrates that this architecture can process sequences more efficiently than Transformers for certain tasks and is theoretically capable of universal computation with minimal depth.
main_problem: The paper addresses the limitations of current sequence modeling architectures in handling long-range dependencies based on value significance rather than temporal recency, and explores the computational expressiveness of a new hysteretic attention mechanism.
main_result: The authors prove that the Preisach Attention Layer (PAL) is Turing-complete at O(1) depth and establish that its function class is incomparable to standard Transformers, specifically excelling at computing historical range statistics via rate-independence.
method: The authors introduce the Preisach Attention Layer (PAL), which replaces softmax attention with a binary relay operator based on the Preisach hysteresis model, and use formal proofs involving 2-stack pushdown automata and Extremum First-Order Logic.
model_or_system: The model is a Preisach Attention Layer (PAL) based on the classical Preisach hysteresis operator, which aggregates binary relays with learned activation/deactivation thresholds. It is also shown to be a sequential, non-equilibrium generalization of the mean-field Random-Field Ising Model (RFIM) at zero temperature.
key_observables: Extremum stack (local maxima and minima), relay activation states, and magnetization-like aggregate outputs.
important_parameters: Activation and deactivation thresholds (alpha, beta), Preisach measure (mu), sequence length (n), and disorder strength (Delta).
main_assumptions: Assumes arbitrary precision arithmetic for Turing completeness proofs and requires smooth sigmoid relaxation of discontinuous relays for practical gradient-based training.
figures_summary: The notes do not provide specific descriptions of figures, but mention tables comparing complexity (Time/Memory) and expressiveness across architectures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the PAL architecture, establishes its Turing completeness via 2-PDA simulation, proves expressiveness separation from Transformers using rate-independence, characterizes its logical framework via Extremum First-Order Logic, and establishes a physical equivalence to the Random-Field Ising Model.
why_interesting: The paper connects neural architecture design to the physics of hysteresis and the Random-Field Ising Model, specifically exploring phase transitions, avalanche dynamics, and the ability of a system to track historical extrema, which is highly relevant to non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
